docs.title: Concepts
docs.text:
Cross-cutting ideas, trends, and frameworks in market research.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: AI Democratization
docs.text: AI democratization refers to the process where AI automates more expert tasks within a job, leaving less expert tasks for humans to perform. This reshaping of work reduces the skill barriers for complex tasks and shifts roles toward less specialized activities.

docs.title: Key Characteristics
docs.text:
In democratized jobs, AI takes over the relatively more expert components of the role, leaving humans with less demanding tasks. For example:

  • Inventory Clerks: AI now performs complex tasks like managing inventory, leaving humans with less expert tasks like moving stock in warehouses
  • Software Developers: AI handles sophisticated coding tasks, leaving humans with more routine implementation work
  • Loan Officers: AI automates complex risk assessment, leaving humans with more standardized processing tasks

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, 52% of jobs globally are being democratized by AI. These democratized jobs show distinct patterns:

  • They are demanding additional skills at a slower rate (33% growth from 2018-2025) compared to professionalized roles (68% growth)
  • They have experienced slower salary growth (26% since 2021) compared to professionalized roles (37%)
  • They are growing more slowly in number (17% growth from 2018-2025) compared to professionalized roles (39% growth)

docs.title: Impact on Entry-Level Roles
docs.text:
Democratization has particular implications for entry-level positions. In democratized roles, AI reduces the expertise required, which can expand the pool of qualified candidates. As David Autor argues, this can enable occupations to grow in number while wages may stagnate.

For example, child care services managers (a democratized role) have seen job listings more than double since 2019 (111% growth) while wages have grown only 8%.

docs.title: Strategic Considerations
docs.text:
Businesses should consider how democratization affects workforce strategy, including potential for expanded talent pools, work redesign, and mobility pathways. Workers in democratized roles may need to focus on developing complementary human-intensive skills to remain competitive.

See also: democratised-jobs, ai-professionalization, human-intensive-skills

docs.title: AI Professionalization
docs.text: AI professionalization refers to the process where AI automates less expert tasks within a job, leaving more expert tasks for humans to perform. This reshaping of work elevates the importance of human expertise, judgment, and creativity in certain roles.

docs.title: Key Characteristics
docs.text:
In professionalized jobs, AI takes over the relatively basic tasks, leaving humans with more complex and expert responsibilities. For example:

  • Recruiters: AI now automatically screens CVs (less expert task), leaving humans to negotiate contracts (more expert task)
  • Radiologists: AI handles basic image analysis, leaving humans to interpret complex cases and make diagnostic decisions
  • Air Traffic Controllers: AI manages routine flight path calculations, leaving humans to handle exceptional situations requiring judgment

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, 22% of jobs globally are being professionalized by AI. These professionalized jobs show distinct advantages in the evolving labor market:

  • They are demanding additional skills at twice the rate of democratized roles (68% growth from 2018-2025 vs. 33%)
  • They have experienced 42% faster salary growth compared to democratized roles (37% vs. 26% since 2021)
  • They are growing more quickly in number (39% growth from 2018-2025 vs. 17% for democratized roles)

docs.title: Impact on Entry-Level Roles
docs.text:
Professionalization creates unique challenges and opportunities for entry-level workers. In professionalized roles, AI increases the expertise required, which can elevate the value of human judgment and creativity.

Entry-level positions in professionalized fields often require workers to quickly develop advanced capabilities, with many now demanding traditionally senior skills much earlier in career progression.

docs.title: Strategic Considerations
docs.text:
Businesses should consider deeper investment in advanced AI fluency, human-intensive skills development, and retention strategies for professionalized roles as demand and wage pressure rises. Workers in these roles should focus on developing complementary human-intensive skills that become more valuable as AI handles routine components of the job.

See also: professionalised-jobs, ai-democratization, human-intensive-skills

docs.title: Seniorised Roles
docs.text: Seniorised roles refer to entry-level positions that have evolved to require skills and capabilities traditionally associated with more senior positions, due to the impact of AI adoption. This phenomenon represents a fundamental shift in early career expectations and pathways.

docs.title: Definition and Identification
docs.text:
A role is classified as "seniorised" when an entry-level position now requires traditionally senior skills that were not previously expected at that career stage. According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer:

  • A skill is defined as "traditionally senior" if it had more than 50 mentions in experienced (non-entry-level), high AI exposure job postings in 2019 but five or fewer mentions in entry-level, high AI exposure postings from the same year
  • An entry-level job posting is classified as "seniorised" if it contains ten or more mentions of skills that are both new and traditionally senior

docs.title: Key Characteristics
docs.text:
Seniorised roles typically require capabilities such as:

  • Motivational leadership
  • Process management
  • Team building
  • Mentorship
  • People management
  • Stakeholder management
  • Data-driven decision making

These capabilities demand emotional intelligence, judgment, and leadership ability at a level not historically required in many junior roles.

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
PwC's analysis reveals significant patterns related to seniorised roles:

  • The most AI-exposed entry-level jobs are seven times more likely to require traditionally senior skills than the least AI-exposed ones
  • For highly AI-exposed entry-level jobs, senior capabilities now account for 52% of new skills required (vs. 7% for least exposed)
  • Seniorised entry-level roles are growing at 35% between 2019-2025, while non-seniorised roles are declining by 10%

docs.title: Impact on the Labor Market
docs.text:
The seniorisation phenomenon has created a divergence in entry-level job trajectories:

  • Entry-level jobs in the highest AI exposure quartile have flatlined since 2022 (coinciding with ChatGPT's release)
  • Only seniorised entry-level roles within high AI exposure fields are experiencing growth
  • Early-career workers must now develop advanced capabilities much earlier in their careers

docs.title: Strategic Implications
docs.text:
For organizations, seniorised roles necessitate: - Redesigned onboarding and training programs - Accelerated mentorship and development pathways - Revised expectations for early-career performance

For workers, particularly those early in their careers: - Opportunity to skip years of routine tasks - Challenge of needing to demonstrate leadership and strategic thinking sooner - Increased importance of developing human-intensive skills alongside technical capabilities

See also: entry-level-jobs, senior-skills, human-intensive-skills, pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer

docs.title: Superstar Effect in AI Adoption
docs.text: The superstar effect refers to the phenomenon where a small subset of companies achieve dramatically outsized benefits from AI adoption compared to their peers. According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, this effect is creating a significant divergence in business performance.

docs.title: Key Findings
docs.text:
While companies with high AI exposure as a whole have seen productivity growth of 33.5% (measured as revenue per employee from 2018-2025), the top 20% of these companies - the "superstars" - have achieved an extraordinary 163% productivity growth rate.

This represents a 5x higher productivity growth rate for the superstar companies compared to the most AI-exposed companies as a whole. The gap has been widening annually since 2022 when AI adoption soared.

docs.title: Headcount and Wage Growth Patterns
docs.text:
The superstar effect extends beyond productivity to workforce metrics:

  • Headcount Growth: The most AI-exposed companies as a whole show 52.2% headcount growth (2018-2025), with superstar companies tracking similarly at approximately 44%
  • Wage Growth: The most AI-exposed companies show 24.4% wage growth, while superstar companies achieve even higher average wage growth of 68%

This pattern contradicts the common narrative that AI primarily reduces headcount, suggesting instead that leading companies are using AI to fuel growth that spurs expansive hiring.

docs.title: What Superstars Do Differently
docs.text:
According to PwC's 2026 AI performance study, the single strongest factor in capturing outsized gains is whether a company uses AI to pursue growth opportunities, especially those created by collaborating or competing across sector lines.

Superstar companies are not using AI only to find efficiencies or reduce headcount. Instead, they are using AI to unlock new revenue, enter new markets, and create new forms of value - what PwC calls "reinvention" rather than just automation.

docs.title: Strategic Implications
docs.text:
For business leaders, the implication is clear: to claim the greatest gains from AI, use it to create new forms of value, not just make old ways of working more efficient. For workers, the implication is to seek opportunities in sectors and companies that are making the most of AI's power to create rather than just automate.

See also: pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, [[pwc-2026-ai-performance-study]]

docs.title: Entities
docs.text:
Companies, research firms, tools, and platforms mentioned.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Lightcast
docs.text: Lightcast (formerly Emsi Burning Glass) is a labor market analytics company that provides detailed data on job postings, skills demand, and workforce trends. The company aggregates and analyzes millions of job postings from across the internet to create comprehensive labor market intelligence.

docs.title: Role in AI Research
docs.text: Lightcast data serves as a primary source for PwC's Global AI Jobs Barometer analysis. According to the 2026 report, the analysis examined over one billion online job advertisements across six continents, with Lightcast providing the underlying job posting data.

docs.title: Data Collection Methodology
docs.text:
Lightcast gathers job posting data through:

  • Web scraping of major job boards and company career sites
  • Partnerships with job posting aggregators
  • Direct feeds from some large employers
  • Historical archives dating back to 2012 for certain markets

The company processes this raw data to extract structured information about required skills, qualifications, compensation, and other job characteristics.

docs.title: Application in PwC's Research
docs.text:
In the PwC 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, Lightcast data was used to:

  • Track changes in job posting volumes across different AI exposure levels
  • Analyze skill requirements and how they evolve over time
  • Calculate wage premiums for AI-related skills
  • Identify trends in entry-level hiring patterns
  • Measure growth in AI specialist positions

docs.title: Key Insights Enabled by Lightcast Data
docs.text:
Analysis of Lightcast data revealed several critical patterns:

  • AI specialist job postings grew eight times faster than total job postings from 2012-2025
  • Entry-level job postings in the highest AI exposure quartile flatlined after 2022
  • Skills needed for the most AI-exposed jobs are changing 2.2x faster than for the least exposed jobs
  • Wage premiums for AI skills reached 62% across all sectors in 2025

docs.title: Limitations and Considerations
docs.text:
While valuable, Lightcast data has certain limitations:

  • Primarily captures formal, online job postings (may miss informal hiring)
  • Coverage varies by country and time period
  • Skill extraction relies on natural language processing of job descriptions
  • May not fully capture internal mobility or promotions

Researchers using Lightcast data, like PwC, typically note these limitations and adjust their methodologies accordingly.

See also: pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, pwc-ai-occupational-exposure-index

docs.title: ORBIS
docs.text: ORBIS is a comprehensive global business information database maintained by Bureau van Dijk (a Moody's Analytics company). It provides detailed financial, ownership, and operational data on millions of companies worldwide, serving as a critical resource for business intelligence and economic research.

docs.title: Role in AI Research
docs.text: In PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, ORBIS data served as the primary source for company-level analysis, particularly regarding productivity, headcount, and wage growth metrics across different levels of AI exposure.

docs.title: Data Coverage
docs.text:
ORBIS includes information on:

  • Over 400 million companies globally
  • Financial statements and performance metrics
  • Ownership structures and corporate hierarchies
  • Industry classifications using standard systems like NAICS
  • Employment data and executive information

For the PwC analysis, the database provided longitudinal data from 2018-2025 for companies with consistent reporting.

docs.title: Application in PwC's Research
docs.text:
PwC used ORBIS data to:

  • Measure productivity growth (revenue per employee) across AI exposure levels
  • Track headcount changes at companies in different AI exposure quartiles
  • Analyze wage growth patterns relative to AI adoption
  • Identify the "superstar effect" among highly AI-exposed companies

docs.title: Key Insights Enabled by ORBIS Data
docs.text:
Analysis of ORBIS data revealed several critical patterns:

  • The most AI-exposed companies showed 33.5% productivity growth (2018-2025) compared to 18.7% for least exposed
  • Headcount growth at most AI-exposed companies reached 52.2% versus 23.3% for least exposed
  • Wage growth at most AI-exposed companies reached 24.4% versus 5.6% for least exposed
  • The top 20% of most AI-exposed companies ("superstars") achieved 163% productivity growth

docs.title: Methodological Considerations
docs.text:
PwC's analysis using ORBIS data included several important methodological notes:

  • Focus on larger formal firms with annual turnover of at least $50m USD
  • Exclusion of companies that exited the market during the period (creating survivorship bias)
  • Potential bias toward larger, more formalized companies in some countries
  • Company AI exposure determined by NAICS sector classification

These considerations mean the analysis reflects patterns among established businesses rather than the entire economy, but still provides valuable insights into how AI adoption correlates with business performance.

See also: pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, superstar-effect

docs.title: PwC
docs.text: PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) is a multinational professional services network and one of the Big Four accounting firms. Founded in 1998 from a merger of Price Waterhouse and Coopers & Lybrand, PwC operates in 155 countries with over 360,000 employees worldwide.

docs.title: Role in Market Research
docs.text: PwC has established itself as a significant contributor to market research, particularly in the intersection of technology, business strategy, and workforce transformation. The firm publishes numerous industry reports and thought leadership pieces that influence business decision-making globally.

docs.title: Key Contributions to AI and Workforce Research
docs.text:
PwC is the publisher of the annual Global AI Jobs Barometer, a comprehensive analysis of AI's impact on the labor market. The 2026 edition analyzed over one billion job advertisements across six continents to identify how AI is reshaping work.

The firm also conducts the Global CEO Survey, which provides insights into executive perspectives on AI adoption and business strategy. According to their 2026 survey, 49% of CEOs expect AI adoption to decrease junior hiring in the next three years.

docs.title: Research Methodology
docs.text:
PwC's workforce research typically combines:

  • Large-scale data analysis (job postings, company performance metrics)
  • Proprietary indices (like the AI Occupational Exposure Index)
  • Framework development (such as the professionalisation/democratisation lens)
  • Cross-referencing with academic research (MIT's EPOCH framework, Teeselink and Carey's expertise analysis)

docs.title: Notable Researchers and Leaders
docs.text:
Key PwC figures involved in AI and workforce research include:

  • Joe Atkinson: Global Chief AI Officer for the PwC Network of Firms
  • Peter Brown: Global Workforce Leader, PwC UK
  • Sarah Brown: Thought Leadership Lead, Global Communications and Change, PwC UK
  • Simon Oates: UK Economics Leader, PwC UK

docs.title: Strategic Positioning
docs.text:
PwC positions itself at the intersection of technology implementation and business strategy, helping organizations navigate AI adoption not just for efficiency but for growth and reinvention. Their research consistently emphasizes that the greatest value from AI comes from using it to create new forms of value rather than simply automating existing processes.

See also: pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, [[pwc-2026-ai-performance-study]]

docs.title: Methodologies
docs.text:
Research methodologies used in literature.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: EPOCH Score Framework
docs.text: The EPOCH score is a measurement framework developed by MIT researchers Loaiza and Rigobon (2025) that assesses the extent to which occupations rely on human capabilities that are resistant to AI automation and complementary to AI systems.

docs.title: Framework Dimensions
docs.text:
EPOCH is an acronym representing five key dimensions of human-intensive capabilities:

  • Empathy: The ability to understand and share the feelings of others, build meaningful connections, and demonstrate compassion
  • Presence: Physical presence, networking abilities, and navigating social contexts to build trust and collaborate effectively
  • Opinion: The capacity to navigate open-ended, ambiguous, or ethical scenarios requiring human judgment and discernment
  • Creativity: Original thinking, improvisation, and the generation of novel ideas and solutions
  • Hope: Grit, perseverance, initiative, and the ability to set strategic direction and inspire others

docs.title: Application in AI Research
docs.text:
According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, the EPOCH framework has been instrumental in understanding how AI reshapes work:

  • New tasks added to AI-exposed roles since 2022 are 2.5x more likely to rely on these human-intensive capabilities
  • The average EPOCH score for new tasks in the most AI-exposed occupations is 0.47, compared to 0.19 for the least exposed
  • Both professionalised roles (0.48) and democratised roles (0.45) show significantly higher EPOCH scores than low exposure roles (0.26)

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
The report shows that occupations with higher AI exposure consistently demonstrate higher EPOCH scores, indicating a growing emphasis on human capabilities as AI handles more routine tasks. This pattern is accelerating, with the gap in human-intensive skill requirements widening over time.

By 2025, the skill transformation gap between high and low AI-exposure occupations reached 116% - meaning the most AI-exposed occupations were evolving their skillsets at more than twice the rate of the least exposed roles.

docs.title: Strategic Implications
docs.text:
The EPOCH framework provides valuable insights for both business leaders and workers: - Organizations should invest in developing these human-intensive capabilities alongside AI skills - Workers should focus on honing these skills to remain competitive in an AI-augmented workplace - Educators should incorporate EPOCH dimensions into curriculum design for future workforce readiness

See also: human-intensive-skills, pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, [[loaiza-and-rigobon]]

docs.title: PwC AI Occupational Exposure Index
docs.text: The PwC AI Occupational Exposure Index is a proprietary methodology developed by PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC) to measure the degree to which different occupations are exposed to AI capabilities. This index serves as a foundational tool for PwC's Global AI Jobs Barometer analysis.

docs.title: Methodology Overview
docs.text:
The index assesses occupational exposure to AI through a multi-dimensional framework that considers:

  1. Task Analysis: Evaluation of specific tasks within occupations to determine which could be automated or augmented by current AI capabilities
  2. Skill Mapping: Analysis of required skills against AI's current capabilities and limitations
  3. Technology Adoption Trends: Incorporation of real-world AI adoption patterns across industries
  4. Expert Assessment: Input from industry specialists on the feasibility of AI implementation in different occupational contexts

docs.title: Application in Research
docs.text:
The PwC AI Occupational Exposure Index is used to:

  • Categorize occupations into quartiles of AI exposure (lowest to highest)
  • Identify professionalised vs. democratised jobs based on how AI changes expertise requirements
  • Track changes in skill demands across different exposure levels over time
  • Analyze company-level performance based on sectoral AI exposure

docs.title: Key Insights from the Index
docs.text:
Analysis using the index has revealed several critical patterns:

  • Skill Transformation Acceleration: The most AI-exposed occupations are evolving their skillsets at more than twice the rate of the least exposed roles, with the gap widening to 116% by 2025
  • Human-Intensive Skill Growth: New tasks added to highly AI-exposed roles are 2.5x more likely to rely on human-intensive capabilities measured by the EPOCH framework
  • Entry-Level Transformation: The highest AI exposure quartile of entry-level jobs has flatlined since 2022, while roles that have been "seniorised" are growing at 35%

docs.title: Differentiation from Other Measures
docs.text:
Unlike simpler automation risk assessments that focus solely on task replacement, the PwC index captures the nuanced ways AI reshapes work:

  • It distinguishes between professionalisation (AI automating less expert tasks) and democratisation (AI automating more expert tasks)
  • It tracks how AI exposure affects not just job numbers but also skill requirements, wages, and career progression
  • It incorporates both technological feasibility and real-world adoption patterns

docs.title: Strategic Value
docs.text:
The index provides valuable insights for multiple stakeholders:

  • Business Leaders: Helps identify which roles are likely to transform most significantly and where to focus workforce development
  • Workers: Guides career decisions by highlighting which occupations are gaining or losing value in the AI era
  • Educators: Informs curriculum development to prepare students for evolving skill requirements
  • Policy Makers: Supports workforce planning and transition programs

See also: pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer, ai-democratization, ai-professionalization, epoch-score

docs.title: Roles
docs.text:
Market research job roles, positions, and career paths.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Ai specialist
docs.text: AI specialists are in high demand, with job postings growing eight times faster than total job postings. This indicates a strong prioritization of AI investment across all sectors.

docs.title: Democratised Jobs
docs.text: Democratised jobs are occupations that have been reshaped by AI to demand less human expertise. In these roles, AI automates the relatively more expert tasks, leaving humans with less demanding responsibilities.

docs.title: Definition and Identification
docs.text:
Democratised jobs are identified through analysis of how AI changes the expertise requirements of occupations. According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, these jobs represent approximately 52% of advertised positions globally.

A job is classified as democratised when AI automation leaves humans performing tasks that require less specialized knowledge or capabilities than before AI adoption.

docs.title: Examples of Democratised Jobs
docs.text:
• Inventory Clerks: AI now performs complex tasks like managing inventory, leaving humans with less expert tasks like moving stock
  • Software Developers: AI handles sophisticated coding tasks, leaving humans with more routine implementation work
  • Loan Officers: AI automates complex risk assessment, leaving humans with more standardized processing tasks
  • Finance Managers: AI performs advanced analytics, leaving humans with more execution-focused tasks
  • Medical Secretaries: AI handles scheduling and basic communications, leaving humans with more routine administrative work

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
Democratised jobs show distinct patterns in the evolving labor market:

  • Job Growth: 17% growth in job postings from 2018-2025, less than half the rate of professionalised jobs (39%)
  • Skill Demand: 33% growth in required skills from 2018-2025, half the rate of professionalised jobs (68%)
  • Wage Growth: 26% growth in advertised salaries since 2021, 42% lower than professionalised jobs (37%)

docs.title: Impact on Entry-Level Opportunities
docs.text:
Democratisation has particular implications for entry-level positions. As David Autor argues, when technology reduces the expertise required for a job, the pool of qualified candidates expands, which can enable the occupation to grow while wages may stagnate.

For example, child care services managers (a democratised role) have seen job listings more than double since 2019 (111% growth) while wages have grown only 8%.

docs.title: Strategic Considerations
docs.text:
Businesses should consider proactive work redesign, mobility pathways, and refreshed career propositions for democratised roles as AI changes the expertise required. Workers in these roles may need to focus on developing complementary human-intensive skills to remain competitive or consider transitioning to roles where AI creates rather than reduces expertise requirements.

See also: ai-democratization, human-intensive-skills, pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer

docs.title: Entry-Level Jobs in the AI Era
docs.text: Entry-level job markets are experiencing significant transformation due to AI adoption. While overall entry-level job postings in quartiles with lower AI exposure continue to grow, those in the highest AI exposure quartile have flatlined since 2022, coinciding with the release of ChatGPT.

docs.title: The Seniorisation Phenomenon
docs.text:
A key finding from PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer is that AI-exposed entry-level roles are rapidly evolving to demand skills traditionally required of senior workers. The most AI-exposed entry-level jobs are now seven times more likely to require traditionally senior skills than the least AI-exposed ones.

Examples of skills traditionally required in senior roles that are now appearing in entry-level positions include: - Motivational leadership - Process management - Team building - Mentorship - People management - Stakeholder management - Data-driven decision making

docs.title: Divergent Growth Patterns
docs.text:
Entry-level roles can be categorized as "seniorised" or "non-seniorised" based on whether they now require traditionally senior capabilities:

  • Seniorised roles: Entry-level positions requiring more than 10 new, traditionally senior skills are thriving with 35% growth between 2019-2025
  • Non-seniorised roles: Other entry-level roles in high AI exposure fields are declining by 10% during the same period

This represents a fundamental shift in early career pathways, requiring companies and educators to rethink how they train, mentor, and scaffold development for junior workers.

docs.title: Strategic Implications
docs.text:
For early-career workers, the good news is being spared years of drudgery on basic, repetitive tasks. The challenge is needing to quickly develop and demonstrate advanced skills like leadership and strategic thinking much earlier in their careers.

Organizations must reinvent early career pathways by redesigning onboarding, mentorship, and training programs to accelerate development of these advanced capabilities.

See also: senior-skills, ai-democratization, ai-professionalization

docs.title: Professionalised Jobs
docs.text: Professionalised jobs are occupations that have been reshaped by AI to demand more human expertise, judgment, and creativity. In these roles, AI automates the relatively basic tasks, leaving humans with more complex and expert responsibilities.

docs.title: Definition and Identification
docs.text:
Professionalised jobs are identified through analysis of how AI changes the expertise requirements of occupations. According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, these jobs represent approximately 22% of advertised positions globally.

A job is classified as professionalised when AI automation leaves humans performing tasks that require more specialized knowledge or capabilities than before AI adoption.

docs.title: Examples of Professionalised Jobs
docs.text:
• Radiologists: AI handles basic image analysis, leaving humans to interpret complex cases and make diagnostic decisions
  • Employment Recruiters: AI automatically screens CVs, leaving humans with more demanding tasks like negotiating contracts
  • Air Traffic Controllers: AI manages routine flight path calculations, leaving humans to handle exceptional situations
  • Financial Analysts: AI performs data gathering and basic modeling, leaving humans to develop strategic insights
  • Nursing Aides: AI tracks vital signs and schedules medication, enabling more time for patient relationship building

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
Professionalised jobs show distinct advantages in the evolving labor market:

  • Job Growth: 39% growth in job postings from 2018-2025, more than twice the rate of democratized jobs (17%)
  • Skill Demand: 68% growth in required skills from 2018-2025, double the rate of democratized jobs (33%)
  • Wage Growth: 37% growth in advertised salaries since 2021, 42% higher than democratized jobs (26%)

docs.title: Impact on Workforce Development
docs.text:
Professionalised jobs are increasingly demanding human-intensive capabilities as outlined in the EPOCH framework. The average EPOCH score for professionalised roles is 0.45, significantly higher than low exposure roles (0.26).

Workers in professionalised roles need to develop complementary skills that become more valuable as AI handles routine components of the job, particularly in areas of judgment, creativity, and strategic thinking.

docs.title: Strategic Considerations
docs.text:
Businesses should consider deeper investment in advanced AI fluency, human-intensive skills development, and retention strategies for professionalised roles as demand and wage pressure rises. Workers in these roles should focus on developing expertise that complements rather than competes with AI capabilities.

See also: ai-professionalization, human-intensive-skills, pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Skills, competencies, and requirements found in job listings.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: Ai skills
docs.text: AI skills are increasingly in demand across various sectors, with wage premiums rising significantly. According to the 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, wage premiums for workers with AI skills have risen to 62%, with Consumer Markets showing the highest premium at 118%.

docs.title: Human-Intensive Skills
docs.text: Human-intensive skills are capabilities that are distinctively human and increasingly valued in AI-exposed roles. According to PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, new tasks added to AI-exposed roles since 2022 are 2.5x more likely to rely on these human-intensive capabilities compared to tasks in less AI-exposed roles.

docs.title: The EPOCH Framework
docs.text:
The EPOCH framework, developed by MIT researchers Loaiza and Rigobon (2025), identifies five key dimensions of human-intensive capabilities that are most resistant to AI automation and complementary to AI systems:

  • Empathy & Emotional Intelligence: Compassion, understanding, and building meaningful connections with others
  • Presence, Networking & Connectedness: Physical presence, networking abilities, and navigating social contexts to build trust
  • Opinion, Judgment & Ethics: Navigating open-ended, ambiguous, or ethical scenarios requiring human discernment
  • Creativity & Imagination: Original thinking, improvisation, and generating novel ideas
  • Hope, Vision & Leadership: Grit, perseverance, initiative, and setting strategic direction

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
The report shows that the average EPOCH score for new tasks in the most AI-exposed occupations is 0.47, compared to 0.19 for the least exposed occupations - a 2.5x difference. Both professionalised and democratised roles show significantly higher EPOCH scores (0.48 and 0.45 respectively) compared to low exposure roles (0.26).

This trend is accelerating, with the skill transformation gap between high and low AI-exposure occupations widening over time. By 2025, the top quartile of AI-exposed occupations shows 116% higher net skill change compared to the bottom quartile.

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
As AI absorbs routine and technical tasks, these human-intensive skills become increasingly competitively valuable. Workers who develop these capabilities alongside AI proficiency will be best positioned for success in the evolving job market. Organizations should prioritize investing in these skills through training and development programs.

See also: epoch-score, ai-professionalization, ai-democratization

docs.title: Senior Skills in Entry-Level Roles
docs.text: Senior skills refer to capabilities traditionally required in more senior positions that are now increasingly appearing in entry-level job descriptions due to AI adoption. This phenomenon, documented in PwC's 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer, represents a significant shift in early career expectations.

docs.title: Definition and Identification
docs.text:
A skill is defined as "traditionally senior" if it had more than 50 mentions in experienced (non-entry-level), high AI exposure job postings in 2019 but five or fewer mentions in entry-level, high AI exposure postings from the same year.

An entry-level job posting is classified as "seniorised" if it contains ten or more mentions of skills that are both new (having more than 10 mentions in 2025 but five or fewer in 2019) and traditionally senior.

docs.title: Key Senior Skills Now Required in Entry-Level Positions
docs.text:
The most common senior skills now appearing in AI-exposed entry-level roles include:

  • Motivational leadership: Inspiring and guiding others toward shared goals
  • Process management: Designing, implementing, and optimizing workflows
  • Team building: Creating cohesive, high-performing groups
  • Mentorship: Guiding and developing less experienced colleagues
  • People management: Overseeing staff performance, development, and engagement
  • Stakeholder management: Building and maintaining relationships with key parties
  • Data-driven decision making: Using analytics to inform strategic choices

docs.title: Statistical Evidence
docs.text:
PwC's analysis reveals that the most AI-exposed entry-level jobs are now seven times more likely to require traditionally senior skills than the least AI-exposed ones. For entry-level jobs highly exposed to AI, these senior capabilities now account for 52% of new skills required, compared to just 7% for entry-level jobs least exposed to AI.

This shift has created a divergence in job growth patterns: - Seniorised entry-level roles: 35% growth between 2019-2025 - Non-seniorised entry-level roles: -10% growth during the same period

docs.title: Strategic Implications
docs.text:
For early-career workers, this represents both opportunity and challenge. The positive aspect is being spared years of routine tasks, but the challenge is needing to develop advanced capabilities much earlier in their careers.

Organizations must reinvent early career pathways by redesigning onboarding, mentorship, and training programs to accelerate development of these advanced skills. Educators should also adapt curricula to prepare students for these evolving expectations.

See also: entry-level-jobs, human-intensive-skills, pwc-2026-global-ai-jobs-barometer

docs.title: Studies
docs.text:
Research studies, papers, and reports on skills gaps.

See Home for the full catalog.

docs.title: PwC 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer
docs.text: The PwC 2026 Global AI Jobs Barometer is a comprehensive analysis of over one billion online job advertisements across six continents, examining how AI is reshaping the global labor market. Published by PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC), this annual report provides critical insights into AI's impact on jobs, skills, and business performance.

docs.title: Two-Track Labor Market
docs.text:
The report identifies a fundamental divergence in how AI affects different types of jobs: - Professionalised jobs (22% of jobs): AI automates less expert tasks, leaving more expert tasks for humans - Democratised jobs (52% of jobs): AI automates more expert tasks, leaving less expert tasks for humans - Low AI exposure jobs (26% of jobs): Jobs with few tasks where AI has capabilities

Professionalised jobs are growing twice as fast as democratised jobs (39% vs. 17% growth from 2018-2025) and seeing 42% higher wage growth since 2021.

docs.title: Entry-Level Transformation
docs.text: The report documents how AI is reshaping entry-level opportunities: - Entry-level jobs in the highest AI exposure quartile have flatlined since 2022 - AI-exposed entry-level roles are rapidly evolving to require traditionally senior capabilities - "Seniorised" entry-level roles (requiring more than 10 new senior skills) are growing at 35%, while non-seniorised roles are declining by 10%

docs.title: Skills Transformation
docs.text: The analysis reveals accelerating skill changes: - Skills needed for the most AI-exposed jobs are changing 2.2x faster than for the least exposed jobs - New tasks added to AI-exposed roles are 2.5x more likely to rely on human-intensive capabilities - The skill transformation gap is widening, reaching 116% by 2025

docs.title: Superstar Effect
docs.text: The report identifies a pronounced "superstar effect" among companies: - The most AI-exposed companies show 33.5% productivity growth (2018-2025) - The top 20% of these companies ("superstars") achieve 163% productivity growth - Superstar companies also show higher wage growth (68% vs. 24.4% for all highly exposed companies)

docs.title: Methodology
docs.text:
The Barometer combines multiple data sources and analytical approaches:

  • Job Posting Analysis: Analysis of over one billion job advertisements from Lightcast
  • AI Exposure Index: PwC's proprietary measure of occupational AI exposure
  • EPOCH Framework: MIT-developed measurement of human-intensive capabilities
  • Company-Level Analysis: ORBIS data on productivity, headcount, and wages

The report tracks changes from 2012-2025, with particular focus on the period since 2022 when generative AI adoption accelerated.

docs.title: Strategic Implications
docs.text:
For business leaders, the report recommends: 1. Using AI to pursue growth opportunities rather than just efficiency 2. Shaping workforce strategy using the professionalised/democratised lens 3. Investing in agentic AI as the ultimate complement to human expertise 4. Reinventing early career pathways for the AI era 5. Building human-intensive skills alongside AI proficiency

For workers, key recommendations include: 1. Moving toward roles made more expert by AI 2. Seeking opportunities in pioneering companies and industries 3. Learning to command AI as a tool and partner 4. Building human-intensive skills like creativity and judgment 5. Developing senior skills rapidly for early-career workers

See also: pwc, ai-democratization, ai-professionalization, superstar-effect, human-intensive-skills
